behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching behavior (recursive watch coverage across directories). A unit/integration fake event emitter can miss real watcher registration gaps and OS event delivery differences for nested paths. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start the daemon against a real temp root directory, create a nested subdirectory, write a file in that subdirectory, and assert the transformed output appears in the output directory exactly once.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: This is explicitly local deterministic policy logic (path+time-window decision). Real OS events are not required to expose the core failure; a fake clock and direct calls catch incorrect window math/state updates. Rejected higher level: behavior text is about "deduplication logic" itself, not watcher/OS interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Test the dedup component/function directly with a controlled clock: first request accepted, second same-path request inside window rejected, third request after window accepted.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS-level duplicate event emission. Mocked watcher events can hide real duplicate patterns/timing produced by the kernel or watch backend. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon with a real watched directory, perform a single write pattern known to trigger multiple FS events, capture processing invocations, and assert exactly one completed processing for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: This requires real OS filesystem lifecycle behavior (directory deletion/recreation invalidating and re-establishing watches). In-process mocks often miss real watcher teardown/rebind edge cases. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, delete the watched directory, recreate it at the same path, write a new file, and assert the daemon processes it without restarting.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Failure is mainly module handoff/orchestration (event intake -> metadata lookup -> queue ordering -> processor). This can be caught by wiring those modules with controlled timestamp fixtures; real OS watcher behavior is not strictly required for this specific ordering rule. Rejected higher level (System): behavior text constrains correctness to timestamp-based ordering, not kernel event semantics or OS-specific watch reliability.
behaviors.test_description: Wire ingest, metadata, queue, and processing modules in-process; inject multiple arrival requests with controlled creation timestamps; assert processing order follows ascending/defined timestamp policy including tie handling.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Real failure depends on OS/filesystem write visibility and timing under concurrent write/read. Mocks typically present atomic writes and can falsely pass. No AI/ML dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Using a real filesystem, begin a slow/chunked write to a file while daemon is running, trigger/observe arrival, verify processing does not start until final chunk/close, then assert processed output matches complete file.
